care.placement_hints: Tolerates low light; drought-tolerant; avoid overwatering.

common_conditions: brown leaf tips from low humidity
lore: Prized in Feng Shui as a protective plant and valued by NASA's Clean Air Study for removing indoor toxins. The stiff, sharp leaves earned it the 'mother-in-law's tongue' nickname.
etymology: Named for Raimondo di Sangro, Prince of San Severo; 'trifasciata' refers to the three-banded cross-striping on its leaves.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: Up to 3 hours morning sun tolerated; avoid harsh afternoon sun
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Slow growth, pale new leaves, floppy stance
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Yellow patches, brown crispy tips in strong afternoon sun

placement_tips: 1–2m from an east or north-facing window
Tolerates bathrooms and hallways with low light
Keep above 13°C; avoid cold windowsills in winter
No humidity requirements — dry room air is fine

conditions.name: Rhizome rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy leaves at the base, leaves falling out of the soil with no root attachment.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, cut away all rotten tissue, dust with cinnamon, let callus 2 days, repot in dry gritty mix.
conditions.prevention: Water only when soil is bone-dry; never water in winter.

conditions.name: Wrinkled curled leaves
conditions.symptoms: Leaves develop vertical creases and curl inward.
conditions.remedy: Deep-water once, then let dry fully. Most cases recover within a week.
conditions.prevention: Don't push waterings past 3 weeks indoors; 4–5 weeks in winter is fine.

conditions.name: Scorched brown patches
conditions.symptoms: Dry brown patches on leaf tips from too much direct sun.
conditions.remedy: Move to bright indirect light; trim damaged tips at an angle.
conditions.prevention: South-facing windowsills in summer often burn leaves — pull back 1m.

conditions.name: Leaning floppy leaves
conditions.symptoms: Tall leaves fall sideways instead of standing upright.
conditions.remedy: Use a stake or rotate the pot weekly. Consider more light.
conditions.prevention: Give at least medium light; rotate quarterly.

conditions.name: Black stem base
conditions.symptoms: Blackening starts at soil line and moves up the leaf.
conditions.remedy: Cut off affected leaves at the soil line; reduce water drastically.
conditions.prevention: Always use a pot with drainage; never top-water heavy.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

conditions.name: Fungus gnats
conditions.symptoms: Small black flies rising from the soil when disturbed; larvae in the top layer.
conditions.remedy: Let the topsoil dry out fully; apply yellow sticky traps; treat with BTI (mosquito bits) if persistent.
conditions.prevention: Avoid constantly wet topsoil; bottom-water when possible.

conditions.name: Spider mites
conditions.symptoms: Fine webbing under leaves, stippled yellow speckling, leaves turn dull.
conditions.remedy: Rinse under the shower, wipe leaves with neem oil or insecticidal soap weekly for 3 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Keep humidity above 50%; inspect new plants before bringing them home.

conditions.name: Mealybugs
conditions.symptoms: White cottony clumps in leaf joints and along stems; sticky residue on leaves.
conditions.remedy: Dab each bug with a cotton swab dipped in 70% isopropyl alcohol; follow up weekly until clear.
conditions.prevention: Quarantine new plants for 2 weeks; wipe leaves monthly.

conditions.name: Scale insects
conditions.symptoms: Small brown bumps stuck to stems or leaf undersides; sticky honeydew underneath.
conditions.remedy: Scrape off by hand, then treat with neem oil every 7 days for a month.
conditions.prevention: Inspect leaf undersides monthly, especially after bringing new plants home.

conditions.name: Fungal leaf spot
conditions.symptoms: Dark or yellow-haloed spots on leaves, sometimes with a concentric pattern.
conditions.remedy: Remove affected leaves, improve airflow, avoid wetting foliage, apply a copper-based fungicide if widespread.
conditions.prevention: Water at the soil, not on leaves; keep plants from crowding each other.

conditions.name: Overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Yellowing leaves that feel soft, consistently damp soil, base of stem blackening.
conditions.remedy: Stop watering, move to brighter spot, check drainage holes, repot if the soil stays wet >10 days.
conditions.prevention: Check soil moisture with your finger before watering; use a pot with drainage.

conditions.name: Underwatering
conditions.symptoms: Crispy leaf edges, drooping, soil pulling away from the pot sides.
conditions.remedy: Soak the pot in a basin for 15 minutes to rehydrate the rootball, then resume a regular schedule.
conditions.prevention: Set a watering reminder; don't skip more than one scheduled watering in a row.

conditions.name: Cold draft damage
conditions.symptoms: Sudden leaf drop, black patches on foliage near a window or door.
conditions.remedy: Move the plant away from drafts and heating vents; trim damaged leaves once growth resumes.
conditions.prevention: Keep plants at least 30 cm from single-glazed windows in winter and away from exterior doors.

conditions.name: Nutrient deficiency
conditions.symptoms: Uniformly pale leaves, weak new growth, older leaves yellowing first.
conditions.remedy: Flush the pot with plain water, then resume feeding with a balanced liquid fertilizer at half strength.
conditions.prevention: Feed every 2–4 weeks during the growing season; refresh the top 2 cm of soil annually.

care.placement_hints: Likes a moss pole or stake; keep away from direct sun.

lore: The fruit tastes like a mix of pineapple and banana — hence 'deliciosa' — but only when fully ripe; unripe it is harshly irritating. Leaf fenestrations are theorised to let rain and light pass through to lower leaves.
etymology: 'Monstera' comes from the Latin 'monstrum' (monstrous or abnormal), a nod to its oversized, hole-punched leaves; 'deliciosa' praises the edible fruit.

light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Leggy growth, small leaves without fenestrations

placement_tips: 1–2m from an east or north-facing window
Avoid cold drafts and heating vents
Prefers 50–60% humidity
Give it a moss pole or stake to climb

conditions.name: No fenestrations (no splits)
conditions.symptoms: New leaves emerge solid, without the signature holes or splits.
conditions.remedy: Move to a brighter spot (bright indirect light) and add a moss pole for the plant to climb.
conditions.prevention: Provide bright indirect light year-round and let the plant climb something vertical.

conditions.name: Yellowing leaves
conditions.symptoms: Older leaves turn uniformly yellow, sometimes with brown tips.
conditions.remedy: Check drainage, let soil dry fully, trim yellow leaves.
conditions.prevention: Water when the top 3 cm of soil is dry; use a chunky aroid mix.

conditions.name: Aerial root drying
conditions.symptoms: Aerial roots turn brown and brittle instead of green.
conditions.remedy: Mist the roots or point them at a moss pole; they'll anchor once they find moisture.
conditions.prevention: Provide a moss pole or redirect roots into the soil.

conditions.name: Sun-scorched leaves
conditions.symptoms: Crispy brown patches in the middle of leaves after direct sun exposure.
conditions.remedy: Move away from direct sun; trim damaged sections.
conditions.prevention: Keep out of direct afternoon sun; bright indirect is the sweet spot.

conditions.name: Weeping leaves (guttation)
conditions.symptoms: Small water droplets at leaf tips in the morning.
conditions.remedy: Harmless — it's normal transpiration. If excessive, cut back water slightly.
conditions.prevention: Don't over-water; Monsteras are vigorous transpirers.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

care.placement_hints: Trailing vine; tolerates neglect; great for shelves and hangers.
care.toxicity: Toxic to pets and humans if ingested.

common_conditions: pale leaves from too much sun
lore: Called 'Devil's ivy' because it is almost impossible to kill and stays green even in the dark. Rarely flowers in cultivation — the only known specimen that did was at the Kew Gardens.
etymology: 'Epipremnum' from Greek 'epi' (upon) and 'premnon' (trunk) — it climbs tree trunks in the wild; 'aureum' means golden, for the yellow variegation.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: None — keep out of direct sun
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Leggy vines, tiny leaves, faded variegation

placement_tips: Anywhere with at least medium indirect light
Perfect for trailing off shelves or bookcases
Tolerates a wide range of humidity
Rotate weekly for even growth

conditions.name: Leggy vines with small leaves
conditions.symptoms: Long gaps between leaves and tiny new foliage.
conditions.remedy: Move to a brighter spot; prune back and propagate the cuttings.
conditions.prevention: Provide medium-to-bright indirect light; rotate weekly.

conditions.name: Limp drooping vines
conditions.symptoms: Whole plant droops dramatically when thirsty.
conditions.remedy: Deep-water once; recovers within 4–8 hours.
conditions.prevention: Water when the top 3 cm is dry; pothos shows thirst dramatically.

conditions.name: Yellow leaves from overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Older leaves yellow uniformly; soil stays soggy.
conditions.remedy: Let soil dry completely; reduce watering frequency.
conditions.prevention: Use a well-draining potting mix and always let soil partially dry.

conditions.name: Faded variegation
conditions.symptoms: New leaves appear more solid green, losing the golden marbling.
conditions.remedy: Move to brighter indirect light. Prune back solid-green vines to encourage variegated growth.
conditions.prevention: Provide bright indirect light; prune reverted sections promptly.

conditions.name: Brown crispy tips
conditions.symptoms: Leaf tips turn brown and papery.
conditions.remedy: Increase humidity; trim tips for cosmetics.
conditions.prevention: Keep away from heating vents; group with other plants in dry rooms.

care.placement_hints: Extremely drought-tolerant; let soil dry fully between waterings.
care.toxicity: Mildly toxic to pets and humans.

common_conditions: brown leaf tips from fluoridated water
stretched growth in very low light
lore: Stores water in potato-like rhizomes, letting it survive months of drought. Only entered the houseplant trade in the 1990s via Dutch nurseries, despite being known botanically for a century.
etymology: The genus name nods to Zamia, a cycad its leaves resemble; 'zamiifolia' simply means 'with Zamia-like leaves'.

light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Stretched, pale stems; leaning toward light

placement_tips: Tolerates low-light corners and windowless offices (with artificial light)
Ideal 1–3m from any window
No humidity requirements; dry rooms are fine
Keep out of cold drafts below 13°C

conditions.name: Yellow stems from overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Whole stems turn yellow and soft starting at the base.
conditions.remedy: Stop watering immediately, let soil dry out fully for 2+ weeks, trim any mushy rhizomes.
conditions.prevention: Water only when soil is fully dry; winter watering can be skipped entirely.

conditions.name: Wrinkled rhizomes
conditions.symptoms: Underground rhizomes look shrivelled when checked.
conditions.remedy: Deep-water once; they plump back up over a few days.
conditions.prevention: Don't stretch waterings past 5–6 weeks, even for the ZZ.

conditions.name: Brown leaf tips from fluoride
conditions.symptoms: Tips of leaflets turn brown and crispy.
conditions.remedy: Switch to filtered or rain water; flush the pot quarterly.
conditions.prevention: Use rain or filtered water; avoid consistently fluoridated tap water.

conditions.name: Stretched etiolated growth
conditions.symptoms: New stems are thin and pale, leaning towards the window.
conditions.remedy: Move closer to the window; rotate the pot every time you water.
conditions.prevention: Even low-light tolerant, the ZZ appreciates medium indirect light.

conditions.name: Leaves dropping leaflets
conditions.symptoms: Individual leaflets yellow and fall off a stem.
conditions.remedy: Check for sudden temperature changes or a recent move; the plant usually stabilises within 2 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Avoid sudden cold drafts and relocating during winter.

care.placement_hints: East-facing window ideal; soak-and-drain watering; orchid bark mix.

common_conditions: no reblooming without cool night temps
lore: Discovered by Carl Blume in 1825, who named it after mistaking the blooms for a cluster of moths at dusk. Modern tissue culture turned what was once a rare orchid into the world's best-selling houseplant.
etymology: From Greek 'phalaina' (moth) and 'opsis' (resembling) — literally 'moth-like'.

light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Dark green leaves, no flower spikes

placement_tips: East-facing window is ideal
Keep away from heating vents and ripening fruit (ethylene)
Night temperature 10°C cooler in autumn triggers reblooming
Humidity 40–60%

conditions.name: Root rot from sitting water
conditions.symptoms: Roots turn brown and mushy; leaves wrinkle despite wet bark.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim all rotten roots with sterilized scissors, repot in fresh orchid bark.
conditions.prevention: Water by soak-and-drain; never let the crown sit in water.

conditions.name: Limp leaves from dehydration
conditions.symptoms: Leaves go soft and floppy; roots look silvery grey.
conditions.remedy: Soak the root ball in room-temp water for 15–20 minutes, let drain fully.
conditions.prevention: Water weekly in summer, every 10–12 days in winter; watch root colour.

conditions.name: Bud blast (dropping buds)
conditions.symptoms: Flower buds yellow and fall off before opening.
conditions.remedy: Move away from drafts, heat sources, and ripening fruit (ethylene); maintain steady temperatures.
conditions.prevention: Keep away from doors, radiators, and fruit bowls while spiking.

conditions.name: No reblooming
conditions.symptoms: Plant grows healthy leaves but no new flower spike for over a year.
conditions.remedy: Provide 10°C cooler night temperatures for 4–6 weeks in autumn to trigger a spike.
conditions.prevention: Place near a window with cool night dip in autumn.

conditions.name: Sunburned leaves
conditions.symptoms: Bleached or purple patches on leaves after sun exposure.
conditions.remedy: Move to filtered light; damaged patches stay but new growth is fine.
conditions.prevention: Bright indirect only; never direct midday sun.

care.placement_hints: Dramatic wilting signals thirst; recovers within hours after watering.

common_conditions: brown leaf tips from tap water minerals
lore: The white 'flower' is actually a modified leaf (a spathe) wrapped around the true flower spike. Symbolically given to mark peace, rebirth, and memorials.
etymology: From Greek 'spathe' (sheath) and 'phyllon' (leaf); 'wallisii' honours Gustav Wallis, the 19th-century plant collector who first brought it to Europe.
placement_tips: 1–2m from a north or east window
Tolerates bathrooms and kitchens
Humidity ≥40%; likes a pebble tray
Use rain or filtered water to avoid brown leaf tips

conditions.name: Dramatic wilting
conditions.symptoms: Whole plant collapses theatrically when thirsty.
conditions.remedy: Water thoroughly; recovers within 2–4 hours.
conditions.prevention: Don't wait for the collapse — water when top 2 cm of soil dry.

conditions.name: Brown leaf tips from tap water
conditions.symptoms: Consistent brown tips on most leaves.
conditions.remedy: Switch to filtered or rain water; trim tips for cosmetics.
conditions.prevention: Use rain or filtered water; flush pot every 2 months.

conditions.name: No blooms
conditions.symptoms: Healthy green leaves but no white spathes appearing.
conditions.remedy: Move to brighter indirect light; feed lightly with a high-phosphorus fertilizer.
conditions.prevention: Provide bright (not direct) light; feed every 4–6 weeks when growing.

conditions.name: Green flowers
conditions.symptoms: New spathes emerge green instead of pure white, or white spathes turn green.
conditions.remedy: Reduce fertilizer; green is normal as flowers age.
conditions.prevention: Don't over-feed; a greenish tint means the flower is past its prime.

conditions.name: Yellow leaves from overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Older leaves yellow uniformly; soil feels wet.
conditions.remedy: Let soil dry halfway; check drainage.
conditions.prevention: Water when the top 2 cm is dry, not on a fixed schedule.

conditions.name: Spider mites
conditions.symptoms: Fine webbing under leaves, stippled yellow speckling, leaves turn dull.
conditions.remedy: Rinse under the shower, wipe leaves with neem oil or insecticidal soap weekly for 3 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Keep humidity above 50%; inspect new plants before bringing them home.

conditions.name: Mealybugs
conditions.symptoms: White cottony clumps in leaf joints and along stems; sticky residue on leaves.
conditions.remedy: Dab each bug with a cotton swab dipped in 70% isopropyl alcohol; follow up weekly until clear.
conditions.prevention: Quarantine new plants for 2 weeks; wipe leaves monthly.

conditions.name: Scale insects
conditions.symptoms: Small brown bumps stuck to stems or leaf undersides; sticky honeydew underneath.
conditions.remedy: Scrape off by hand, then treat with neem oil every 7 days for a month.
conditions.prevention: Inspect leaf undersides monthly, especially after bringing new plants home.

conditions.name: Fungal leaf spot
conditions.symptoms: Dark or yellow-haloed spots on leaves, sometimes with a concentric pattern.
conditions.remedy: Remove affected leaves, improve airflow, avoid wetting foliage, apply a copper-based fungicide if widespread.
conditions.prevention: Water at the soil, not on leaves; keep plants from crowding each other.

conditions.name: Overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Yellowing leaves that feel soft, consistently damp soil, base of stem blackening.
conditions.remedy: Stop watering, move to brighter spot, check drainage holes, repot if the soil stays wet >10 days.
conditions.prevention: Check soil moisture with your finger before watering; use a pot with drainage.

conditions.name: Underwatering
conditions.symptoms: Crispy leaf edges, drooping, soil pulling away from the pot sides.
conditions.remedy: Soak the pot in a basin for 15 minutes to rehydrate the rootball, then resume a regular schedule.
conditions.prevention: Set a watering reminder; don't skip more than one scheduled watering in a row.

conditions.name: Cold draft damage
conditions.symptoms: Sudden leaf drop, black patches on foliage near a window or door.
conditions.remedy: Move the plant away from drafts and heating vents; trim damaged leaves once growth resumes.
conditions.prevention: Keep plants at least 30 cm from single-glazed windows in winter and away from exterior doors.

conditions.name: Nutrient deficiency
conditions.symptoms: Uniformly pale leaves, weak new growth, older leaves yellowing first.
conditions.remedy: Flush the pot with plain water, then resume feeding with a balanced liquid fertilizer at half strength.
conditions.prevention: Feed every 2–4 weeks during the growing season; refresh the top 2 cm of soil annually.

care.placement_hints: Bottom-water to keep crowns dry; consistent temps around 20 C.

common_conditions: crown rot from water on leaves
lore: Discovered in 1892 by Baron Walter von Saint Paul-Illaire in colonial East Africa. Thousands of cultivars now exist; wild populations are critically endangered.
etymology: Named for Saint Paul-Illaire; 'ionantha' means 'violet-flowered' from Greek 'ion' (violet) and 'anthos' (flower).
placement_tips: North or east-facing windowsill
Consistent temperature around 20°C
Bottom-water only to keep crown dry
Use warm (not cold) water to avoid ring spots

conditions.name: Crown rot from water on leaves
conditions.symptoms: Central crown turns black and mushy after water splashes.
conditions.remedy: Remove affected plant; propagate a healthy leaf if the crown is fully rotten.
conditions.prevention: Bottom-water only; keep leaves and crown completely dry.

conditions.name: Ring spots from cold water
conditions.symptoms: Ring-shaped pale marks on leaves where cold water touched.
conditions.remedy: Use tepid water; trim disfigured leaves.
conditions.prevention: Always water at room temperature.

conditions.name: No blooms
conditions.symptoms: Plant grows leaves but refuses to flower.
conditions.remedy: Move to bright indirect light (north or east window); feed African Violet fertilizer every 2 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Provide 12+ hours of bright indirect light and consistent feeding.

conditions.name: Powdery mildew
conditions.symptoms: White powdery coating on leaves in humid air.
conditions.remedy: Improve airflow; spray a 1:10 milk-water solution weekly.
conditions.prevention: Don't crowd plants; water the soil only.

conditions.name: Leaves reaching upward
conditions.symptoms: Petioles stretch straight up instead of staying flat.
conditions.remedy: Move closer to a light source.
conditions.prevention: Keep within 30 cm of a bright window.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

conditions.name: Fungus gnats
conditions.symptoms: Small black flies rising from the soil when disturbed; larvae in the top layer.
conditions.remedy: Let the topsoil dry out fully; apply yellow sticky traps; treat with BTI (mosquito bits) if persistent.
conditions.prevention: Avoid constantly wet topsoil; bottom-water when possible.

care.placement_hints: Loves humidity; chunky aroid mix; keep off cold windowsills.

common_conditions: brown leaf tips from dry air
no new flowers in low light
lore: The waxy red 'flower' is a spathe; the yellow spike is the spadix holding hundreds of tiny true flowers. In Hawai'i it's called the 'painter's palette' and symbolises hospitality.
etymology: From Greek 'anthos' (flower) and 'oura' (tail), after the tail-like spadix; 'andraeanum' honours French botanist Édouard André.

light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Few or no flowers; dark leaves

placement_tips: 1m from an east or south-facing window
Humidity ≥50% — pebble tray or humidifier
Keep above 18°C; off cold winter windowsills
Use a chunky aroid mix with bark and perlite

conditions.name: Yellow leaves from overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Leaves yellow from the base up; roots look brown and mushy.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim rotten roots, repot in chunky aroid mix.
conditions.prevention: Use a coarse aroid mix; let top 3 cm dry between waterings.

conditions.name: Brown leaf tips from dry air
conditions.symptoms: Leaf tips turn papery brown.
conditions.remedy: Raise humidity (pebble tray, humidifier, or grouping).
conditions.prevention: Maintain humidity ≥50%; keep away from heating vents.

conditions.name: No new flowers
conditions.symptoms: Healthy foliage but no red spathes for months.
conditions.remedy: Move to brighter indirect light; feed with high-phosphorus fertilizer every 6–8 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Bright indirect light and regular feeding trigger flowering.

conditions.name: Green flowers
conditions.symptoms: New spathes emerge green or fade green quickly.
conditions.remedy: Normal as they age; reduce fertilizer if new spathes are green.
conditions.prevention: Don't over-feed with nitrogen; spathes naturally green with age.

conditions.name: Black leaf edges from cold
conditions.symptoms: Leaf edges blacken after the plant sits near a cold window.
conditions.remedy: Move away from single-glazed windows in winter; trim damaged edges.
conditions.prevention: Keep above 18°C; off cold winter windowsills.

care.placement_hints: South-facing windowsill; gritty succulent mix; minimal water in winter.
care.toxicity: Mildly toxic to pets; topical gel is safe for humans.

common_conditions: brown tips from sunburn when moved suddenly
lore: Used medicinally for at least 6,000 years — mentioned on Egyptian papyrus, by Dioscorides, and in Ayurveda. Cleopatra reportedly applied the gel daily as a beauty treatment.
etymology: 'Aloe' from Arabic 'alloeh' (shining bitter substance) referring to the sap; 'vera' is Latin for 'true' — the authentic medicinal species.

light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Orange-red stress colour (usually desirable), brown tips if moved suddenly

placement_tips: South-facing windowsill preferred
Outdoors in summer once acclimated
Dry room air is fine; no humidity needs
Terracotta pot with gritty succulent mix

conditions.name: Mushy translucent leaves
conditions.symptoms: Leaves feel soft and water-filled, sometimes weeping at the base.
conditions.remedy: Stop watering; unpot to check roots, trim rotten parts, repot in dry gritty mix.
conditions.prevention: Water only when soil is bone-dry; use terracotta for airflow.

conditions.name: Brown tips from sunburn
conditions.symptoms: Leaf tips turn brown after moving to a much brighter spot.
conditions.remedy: Acclimatize gradually; damaged tips stay but new growth is fine.
conditions.prevention: Move Aloe to direct sun slowly over 2 weeks.

conditions.name: Stretched etiolated rosette
conditions.symptoms: Leaves elongate and lean toward the window; pale green.
conditions.remedy: Move to direct sun; rotate weekly.
conditions.prevention: Give a south-facing window or grow light in winter.

conditions.name: Scale insects
conditions.symptoms: Small brown bumps on leaves, especially near the base.
conditions.remedy: Scrape off, wipe with diluted isopropyl alcohol.
conditions.prevention: Quarantine new succulents; inspect monthly.

conditions.name: Red or orange leaves
conditions.symptoms: Whole plant reddens.
conditions.remedy: Normal stress colour from strong sun or cold; no action needed unless combined with shrivel.
conditions.prevention: Aloe colour-stresses beautifully in full sun — embrace it.

care.placement_hints: Unlike most succulents, prefers bright indirect over direct sun.

common_conditions: pale closed rosette in low light
lore: In its native habitat it hides between rocks and grasses, which is why it scorches in the full sun other succulents enjoy. Reclassified from Haworthia to Haworthiopsis in 2013 based on DNA sequencing.
etymology: Honours British botanist Adrian Hardy Haworth; 'attenuata' means 'tapering' for the slender leaf tips.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: Max 2 hours morning sun; full direct sun bleaches

placement_tips: East-facing windowsill ideal
Not the classic "full sun" succulent — prefers bright indirect
Dry room air is fine
Terracotta pot with gritty mix

conditions.name: Soft mushy leaves
conditions.symptoms: Leaves lose rigidity and feel water-logged.
conditions.remedy: Stop watering; let soil dry fully for 2+ weeks.
conditions.prevention: Water every 3 weeks max; gritty mix only.

conditions.name: Red purple leaf stress
conditions.symptoms: Leaves take on a red-purple tint in strong sun.
conditions.remedy: Normal sun stress; move to bright indirect if extreme.
conditions.prevention: Bright indirect is the sweet spot; direct sun OK with gradual acclimation.

conditions.name: Pale closed rosette
conditions.symptoms: Rosette closes up and leaves look pale.
conditions.remedy: Move to brighter light.
conditions.prevention: Bright indirect light year-round.

conditions.name: Black root rot
conditions.symptoms: Plant wobbles in its pot; base feels mushy.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, remove all rotten roots, let callus 3 days, repot dry.
conditions.prevention: Never sit in water; use a pot with large drainage holes.

conditions.name: Offsets crowding the pot
conditions.symptoms: Many baby plants around the mother, crowding the soil.
conditions.remedy: Separate offsets at repotting; they root easily.
conditions.prevention: Repot every 2–3 years; share the babies with friends.

care.placement_hints: Full sun for tight rosette; water from below to keep leaves dry.

common_conditions: sunburn when suddenly moved to direct sun
lore: Each rosette is a single plant; once it flowers and dies, it leaves behind 'pups' that take its place. The pale farina coating (epicuticular wax) acts as natural sunscreen and must not be rubbed off.
etymology: Named after 18th-century Mexican botanical illustrator Atanasio Echeverría y Godoy; 'elegans' simply means elegant.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: 4–6 hours direct sun for a tight rosette
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Sunburn patches if moved suddenly to strong sun

placement_tips: South or west-facing windowsill
Outdoors in summer once acclimated
Terracotta with gritty succulent mix
Water from below to keep rosette dry

conditions.name: Stretched (etiolated) rosette
conditions.symptoms: Stem lengthens and leaves space out, losing the tight rosette.
conditions.remedy: Behead the plant, let callus 3 days, replant the top in gritty mix.
conditions.prevention: Provide direct sun; south-facing window is ideal.

conditions.name: Crown rot from water in rosette
conditions.symptoms: Center of the rosette blackens after water pooled there.
conditions.remedy: If the core is mushy, take healthy leaves and propagate.
conditions.prevention: Water at the soil only; avoid misting.

conditions.name: Mealybugs in rosette folds
conditions.symptoms: White cotton-like clumps between inner leaves.
conditions.remedy: Dab with alcohol-dipped cotton swab between every leaf.
conditions.prevention: Inspect monthly; isolate new echeveria for 2 weeks.

conditions.name: Sunburn from sudden sun
conditions.symptoms: Leaves develop white patches after a move to strong sun.
conditions.remedy: Acclimatize gradually over 2 weeks; trim disfigured leaves.
conditions.prevention: Always move succulents into direct sun gradually.

conditions.name: Bottom leaves shrivelling
conditions.symptoms: Oldest leaves go papery and fall off.
conditions.remedy: Normal — pull them off gently to prevent pests hiding underneath.
conditions.prevention: Nothing to prevent; it's the rosette growing upward.

care.placement_hints: Develops red leaf margins in full sun; prune for bonsai-like shape.

lore: A Feng Shui classic — the jade-green coin-shaped leaves symbolise wealth, and a jade plant at a shop entrance is said to welcome prosperity. Long-lived specimens form gnarled bonsai-like trunks.
etymology: 'Crassula' from Latin 'crassus' (thick), referring to the fleshy leaves; 'ovata' means egg-shaped.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: 4+ hours direct sun brings out red leaf edges
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Occasional leaf drop if moved suddenly into hot sun

placement_tips: South-facing windowsill
Keep above 10°C; off cold winter windowsills
Dry air is fine; no humidity needed
Terracotta pot, water sparingly in winter

conditions.name: Leaf drop from cold drafts
conditions.symptoms: Whole leaves drop suddenly after a cold snap near a window.
conditions.remedy: Move away from cold glass; plant rebounds in spring.
conditions.prevention: Keep above 10°C in winter; avoid single-glazed windowsills.

conditions.name: Soft mushy trunk base
conditions.symptoms: The thick trunk feels soft and discolours near the soil line.
conditions.remedy: Check drainage; trim healthy top, propagate it, discard the rotten base.
conditions.prevention: Water sparingly, especially in winter; use a gritty mix.

conditions.name: Powdery mildew
conditions.symptoms: White powdery coating on leaves.
conditions.remedy: Improve airflow; treat with a milk-water spray or potassium bicarbonate.
conditions.prevention: Don't crowd plants; avoid wetting leaves.

conditions.name: Red leaf margins
conditions.symptoms: Leaf edges blush red in strong sun.
conditions.remedy: Normal and prized sun-stress colour.
conditions.prevention: Give direct sun to develop the red edges.

conditions.name: Shrivelled leaves
conditions.symptoms: Leaves look wrinkled and thin.
conditions.remedy: Deep-water once; plumps up within days.
conditions.prevention: Don't stretch waterings past 3 weeks in summer.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

conditions.name: Fungus gnats
conditions.symptoms: Small black flies rising from the soil when disturbed; larvae in the top layer.
conditions.remedy: Let the topsoil dry out fully; apply yellow sticky traps; treat with BTI (mosquito bits) if persistent.
conditions.prevention: Avoid constantly wet topsoil; bottom-water when possible.

conditions.name: Spider mites
conditions.symptoms: Fine webbing under leaves, stippled yellow speckling, leaves turn dull.
conditions.remedy: Rinse under the shower, wipe leaves with neem oil or insecticidal soap weekly for 3 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Keep humidity above 50%; inspect new plants before bringing them home.

conditions.name: Mealybugs
conditions.symptoms: White cottony clumps in leaf joints and along stems; sticky residue on leaves.
conditions.remedy: Dab each bug with a cotton swab dipped in 70% isopropyl alcohol; follow up weekly until clear.
conditions.prevention: Quarantine new plants for 2 weeks; wipe leaves monthly.

conditions.name: Scale insects
conditions.symptoms: Small brown bumps stuck to stems or leaf undersides; sticky honeydew underneath.
conditions.remedy: Scrape off by hand, then treat with neem oil every 7 days for a month.
conditions.prevention: Inspect leaf undersides monthly, especially after bringing new plants home.

conditions.name: Fungal leaf spot
conditions.symptoms: Dark or yellow-haloed spots on leaves, sometimes with a concentric pattern.
conditions.remedy: Remove affected leaves, improve airflow, avoid wetting foliage, apply a copper-based fungicide if widespread.
conditions.prevention: Water at the soil, not on leaves; keep plants from crowding each other.

conditions.name: Overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Yellowing leaves that feel soft, consistently damp soil, base of stem blackening.
conditions.remedy: Stop watering, move to brighter spot, check drainage holes, repot if the soil stays wet >10 days.
conditions.prevention: Check soil moisture with your finger before watering; use a pot with drainage.

conditions.name: Underwatering
conditions.symptoms: Crispy leaf edges, drooping, soil pulling away from the pot sides.
conditions.remedy: Soak the pot in a basin for 15 minutes to rehydrate the rootball, then resume a regular schedule.
conditions.prevention: Set a watering reminder; don't skip more than one scheduled watering in a row.

conditions.name: Cold draft damage
conditions.symptoms: Sudden leaf drop, black patches on foliage near a window or door.
conditions.remedy: Move the plant away from drafts and heating vents; trim damaged leaves once growth resumes.
conditions.prevention: Keep plants at least 30 cm from single-glazed windows in winter and away from exterior doors.

conditions.name: Nutrient deficiency
conditions.symptoms: Uniformly pale leaves, weak new growth, older leaves yellowing first.
conditions.remedy: Flush the pot with plain water, then resume feeding with a balanced liquid fertilizer at half strength.
conditions.prevention: Feed every 2–4 weeks during the growing season; refresh the top 2 cm of soil annually.

care.placement_hints: Epiphytic — unlike desert cacti wants richer mix and regular water; needs cool dark nights to set buds.

common_conditions: no blooms without cool fall nights
lore: Heirloom specimens are often passed down for a century or more; cuttings root so easily that a single plant can seed an entire family. Unlike desert cacti, it grows on trees in Brazilian cloud forests.
etymology: Named for Belgian plantsman Frédéric Schlumberger; 'truncata' means cut off, describing the squared-off edges of each leaf segment.
placement_tips: East or north-facing window
Needs 12–14 hours of darkness and cool nights in autumn to set buds
Don't move the plant once buds form
Epiphytic mix with bark and perlite

conditions.name: Bud drop from location change
conditions.symptoms: Flower buds fall off after being moved to a new spot.
conditions.remedy: Don't move while budding; place in its flowering spot in autumn and leave it there.
conditions.prevention: Pick one spot for the blooming season and don't move the plant.

conditions.name: Limp wrinkled segments
conditions.symptoms: Stem segments turn soft and thin.
conditions.remedy: Water thoroughly; recovers within 2–3 days.
conditions.prevention: Treat as tropical, not desert — water more than a typical cactus.

conditions.name: Root rot from heavy soil
conditions.symptoms: Whole plant wilts despite wet soil; base turns black.
conditions.remedy: Repot in a chunky orchid-bark-heavy mix.
conditions.prevention: Use an epiphytic mix with bark and perlite, not cactus mix.

conditions.name: No blooms
conditions.symptoms: Healthy plant but no flower buds form in autumn.
conditions.remedy: Give 12–14 hours of uninterrupted darkness and cool nights (10–15°C) for 6 weeks starting in September.
conditions.prevention: Place in a cool, dark room at night in autumn; no artificial light at night.

conditions.name: Stem segments dropping
conditions.symptoms: Whole segments snap off at the joint.
conditions.remedy: Usually stress from temperature swings or overwatering; propagate the fallen segments.
conditions.prevention: Keep consistent temperature and watering; avoid repotting while budding.

lore: Called the 'compass barrel' because mature plants lean toward the southwest, following the afternoon sun. Apache and Tohono O'odham peoples historically used the pulp as an emergency water source and the flesh to make candy.
etymology: 'Ferocactus' from Latin 'ferox' (fierce), for the formidable spines; 'wislizeni' honours 19th-century German-American naturalist Friedrich Wislizenus.

light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Etiolated pale tip, loss of coloration
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Rare — this cactus thrives in intense desert sun

placement_tips: South-facing windowsill all year
Outdoors in full sun in summer
Gritty mineral mix; no organic matter
No water at all November–February

conditions.name: Basal rot from winter watering
conditions.symptoms: Base turns soft and discolours; plant wobbles.
conditions.remedy: Stop water entirely; if caught early, repot in dry gritty mix.
conditions.prevention: No water at all November–February; even less if room is cool.

conditions.name: Etiolated tip
conditions.symptoms: Top of the cactus grows narrow and pale.
conditions.remedy: Move to the sunniest spot; nothing reverses the etiolation but new growth will be normal.
conditions.prevention: Full direct sun year-round; south window or outdoors in summer.

conditions.name: Corky scarring
conditions.symptoms: Brown corky patches form on the surface.
conditions.remedy: Cosmetic only; nothing to fix.
conditions.prevention: Avoid physical damage from handling.

conditions.name: Scale on ribs
conditions.symptoms: Tan bumps hide between the ribs.
conditions.remedy: Remove with tweezers; dab with alcohol.
conditions.prevention: Inspect quarterly between the ribs.

conditions.name: Pale yellowing green
conditions.symptoms: Whole cactus turns pale yellow-green.
conditions.remedy: Check for rot at the base; if firm, feed at half strength and wait out the winter.
conditions.prevention: Feed lightly in spring; ensure full sun.

care.placement_hints: Clumping habit; terracotta pot helps dry out between waterings.

common_conditions: pale elongated growth from low light
no blooms without cold dry winter rest
lore: Mammillaria is the largest genus in the cactus family, with over 200 species. Mature clumps reliably ring themselves with a crown of small pink or white flowers every spring.
etymology: 'Mammillaria' from Latin 'mammilla' (nipple), referring to the rounded tubercles that cover the stem instead of ribs; 'elongata' notes the lengthening finger-like stems.
placement_tips: South or west-facing windowsill
Terracotta pot with gritty mix
Cold dry winter rest (5–10°C) triggers spring blooms
No water from November–February

conditions.name: Rot from damp cool conditions
conditions.symptoms: Soft patches appear on one of the clumping stems.
conditions.remedy: Cut off the rotten stem at the base; let cut surface dry.
conditions.prevention: Water sparingly in summer only; never water below 10°C.

conditions.name: Pale elongated growth
conditions.symptoms: New stems are pale and narrower than old ones.
conditions.remedy: Move to direct sun.
conditions.prevention: Full sun, especially a south window.

conditions.name: Red spider mites
conditions.symptoms: Tiny red dots crawling on the tips; dusty look.
conditions.remedy: Rinse with water; follow with miticide or neem weekly for 3 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Raise humidity slightly in hot dry summers.

conditions.name: No blooms
conditions.symptoms: Mature plant never produces flowers.
conditions.remedy: Give a cold dry winter rest (5–10°C, no water) to trigger spring blooms.
conditions.prevention: Cool winter rest is non-negotiable for flowering cacti.

conditions.remedy: Natural aging; no action needed unless soft.
conditions.prevention: Normal on mature clusters.

common_conditions: brown dark spots from root rot
leaf drop from moving or drafts
lore: Became a design-magazine cliché in the 2010s — from near obscurity to ubiquity in under a decade. In its West African home it can reach 15 metres and begins life as a 'strangler' on a host tree.
etymology: 'Ficus' is the classical Latin name for the fig; 'lyrata' means lyre-shaped, after the violin-body outline of each leaf.

light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Small pale new leaves, dropping lower leaves
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Scorched brown patches in the middle of leaves

Don't move once settled — fiddles hate change

conditions.name: Brown crispy leaf edges

conditions.remedy: Check the watering routine — usually underwatering or inconsistent schedule.
conditions.prevention: Water consistently when top 3 cm is dry; never let it bone-dry.

conditions.name: Brown dark spots from root rot

conditions.prevention: Use a pot with drainage; don't water on a fixed schedule.

conditions.name: Leaf drop from moving
conditions.symptoms: Healthy leaves drop after the plant is moved to a new room.
conditions.remedy: Leave it alone for 4–6 weeks; new leaves will grow.
conditions.prevention: Pick a spot and don't move it; fiddles hate change.

conditions.name: Dusty dull leaves
conditions.symptoms: Leaves look dusty and less glossy.
conditions.remedy: Wipe leaves with a damp cloth monthly.
conditions.prevention: Wipe leaves during monthly plant-care day.

conditions.name: Bare lower stem
conditions.symptoms: Plant keeps losing lower leaves, leaving a bare stem.
conditions.remedy: Prune the top to encourage branching; air-layer the top if you want a compact shape.
conditions.prevention: Provide bright indirect light and consistent watering; bare stems are normal with age.

conditions.remedy: Scrape off by hand, then treat with neem oil every 7 days for a month.
conditions.prevention: Inspect leaf undersides monthly, especially after bringing new plants home.

conditions.name: Fungal leaf spot
conditions.symptoms: Dark or yellow-haloed spots on leaves, sometimes with a concentric pattern.
conditions.remedy: Remove affected leaves, improve airflow, avoid wetting foliage, apply a copper-based fungicide if widespread.
conditions.prevention: Water at the soil, not on leaves; keep plants from crowding each other.

conditions.name: Overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Yellowing leaves that feel soft, consistently damp soil, base of stem blackening.
conditions.remedy: Stop watering, move to brighter spot, check drainage holes, repot if the soil stays wet >10 days.
conditions.prevention: Check soil moisture with your finger before watering; use a pot with drainage.

conditions.name: Underwatering
conditions.symptoms: Crispy leaf edges, drooping, soil pulling away from the pot sides.
conditions.remedy: Soak the pot in a basin for 15 minutes to rehydrate the rootball, then resume a regular schedule.
conditions.prevention: Set a watering reminder; don't skip more than one scheduled watering in a row.

conditions.name: Cold draft damage
conditions.symptoms: Sudden leaf drop, black patches on foliage near a window or door.
conditions.remedy: Move the plant away from drafts and heating vents; trim damaged leaves once growth resumes.
conditions.prevention: Keep plants at least 30 cm from single-glazed windows in winter and away from exterior doors.

conditions.name: Nutrient deficiency
conditions.symptoms: Uniformly pale leaves, weak new growth, older leaves yellowing first.
conditions.remedy: Flush the pot with plain water, then resume feeding with a balanced liquid fertilizer at half strength.
conditions.prevention: Feed every 2–4 weeks during the growing season; refresh the top 2 cm of soil annually.

care.placement_hints: Classic Victorian parlour palm; slow-growing but long-lived.

common_conditions: brown tips from dry air or fluoride
lore: Native only to Lord Howe Island — a speck of land in the Tasman Sea. The island's economy has relied on exporting Kentia palm seeds for over a century, each painstakingly hand-collected and certified.
etymology: 'Howea' names the island; 'forsteriana' honours German naturalist Johann Reinhold Forster who sailed with Captain Cook's second voyage.
placement_tips: 1–3m from a window
Humidity ≥40%
Slow-growing; patience required
Use rain or filtered water to avoid tip browning

conditions.name: Brown tips from dry air or fluoride
conditions.symptoms: Frond tips turn brown and brittle.
conditions.remedy: Switch to filtered water; raise humidity slightly.
conditions.prevention: Use rain or filtered water; flush pot quarterly.

conditions.name: Yellow fronds from overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Lower fronds yellow and base of plant stays wet.
conditions.remedy: Let soil dry halfway; check drainage.
conditions.prevention: Water thoroughly then let partly dry; Kentias are drought-tolerant once settled.

conditions.name: Spider mites
conditions.symptoms: Stippled fronds and fine webbing.
conditions.remedy: Rinse and treat with neem oil weekly.
conditions.prevention: Wipe fronds monthly; inspect undersides.

conditions.name: Slow growth
conditions.symptoms: Very few new fronds each year.
conditions.remedy: Normal — Kentias grow ~1 frond per stem per year.
conditions.prevention: Expected; Kentias are slow but long-lived.

conditions.name: Sunburn from direct sun
conditions.symptoms: Fronds bleach and develop brown patches in strong sun.
conditions.remedy: Move to bright indirect; trim damaged fronds.
conditions.prevention: Bright indirect only; avoid south-facing direct sun.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

conditions.name: Fungus gnats
conditions.symptoms: Small black flies rising from the soil when disturbed; larvae in the top layer.
conditions.remedy: Let the topsoil dry out fully; apply yellow sticky traps; treat with BTI (mosquito bits) if persistent.
conditions.prevention: Avoid constantly wet topsoil; bottom-water when possible.

conditions.name: Spider mites
conditions.symptoms: Fine webbing under leaves, stippled yellow speckling, leaves turn dull.
conditions.remedy: Rinse under the shower, wipe leaves with neem oil or insecticidal soap weekly for 3 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Keep humidity above 50%; inspect new plants before bringing them home.

conditions.name: Mealybugs
conditions.symptoms: White cottony clumps in leaf joints and along stems; sticky residue on leaves.
conditions.remedy: Dab each bug with a cotton swab dipped in 70% isopropyl alcohol; follow up weekly until clear.
conditions.prevention: Quarantine new plants for 2 weeks; wipe leaves monthly.

care.placement_hints: Thirstier than most palms; wants consistently moist but not soggy soil.

common_conditions: brown tips from underwatering or tap water
lore: Endangered in its Madagascar homeland but one of the most widely cultivated palms on Earth — the houseplant you're watering is far more common than the wild one. Clumping habit makes it look fuller than most indoor palms.
etymology: 'Dypsis' is of uncertain origin, possibly from Greek 'dypto' (to dive); 'lutescens' means 'becoming yellow' after the golden yellow leaf sheaths and stems.

Humidity ≥50% — critical for Arecas
Repot every 2 years — outgrows fast
Use rain or filtered water

conditions.name: Yellow fronds from overwatering

conditions.name: Fluoride damage

conditions.name: Root-bound decline

care.placement_hints: Riverbank palm — wants lots of light and lots of water; struggles in average living rooms.

common_conditions: brown crispy fronds from low humidity
rapid decline in dry heated rooms
lore: Grows wild only along a handful of Madagascan rivers and is classified as Vulnerable in the wild — yet is one of the most commonly bulk-sold palms at big-box stores, where it rarely thrives indoors.
etymology: 'Ravenea' honours French botanist Louis Ravené; 'rivularis' means 'of the streams', after its riverbank habitat.

light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Bleached patches in hottest afternoon sun

placement_tips: Bright spot by a south or east window
Humidity ≥55% — difficult in heated living rooms
Never let roots dry out fully
Not suitable for most indoor environments long-term

conditions.name: Brown crispy fronds from low humidity
conditions.symptoms: Fronds turn brown and brittle in dry heated rooms.
conditions.remedy: Raise humidity dramatically; move to a bathroom or add a humidifier.
conditions.prevention: Maintain humidity ≥55%; not suitable for dry living rooms.

conditions.name: Yellowing from under-light

conditions.name: Rapid decline in dry heated rooms

conditions.remedy: Relocate to a humid bright spot; trim dead fronds.
conditions.prevention: Only buy a Majesty palm if you can provide high humidity year-round.

common_conditions: rot from water trapped in base
lore: Blushes bright red or purple when it's about to flower — a single event at the end of a rosette's life, after which it produces pups before dying. Needs no soil at all; absorbs water through microscopic leaf scales called trichomes.
etymology: 'Tillandsia' was dedicated to 17th-century Swedish botanist Elias Tillandz; 'ionantha' means 'violet-flowered'.

light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Faded grey-green colour, no blush before blooming

placement_tips: Hang or rest in an airy open dish
Bright spot with good airflow
Soak 20–30 min weekly; dry upside-down

conditions.name: Rot from water trapped in base
conditions.symptoms: Base turns brown and mushy.
conditions.remedy: Too late to save if mushy; propagate any pups that have formed.
conditions.prevention: Always dry upside-down within 2 hours of soaking.

conditions.name: Browning tips from dry air
conditions.symptoms: Leaf tips curl and brown.
conditions.remedy: Soak weekly for 30 minutes; raise humidity.

conditions.name: Mushy base after soaking
conditions.symptoms: Base feels soft after a recent soak.
conditions.remedy: Increase drying time dramatically; check for hidden water.
conditions.prevention: Shake out thoroughly; dry upside-down in a bright airy spot.

conditions.name: No pups after blooming
conditions.symptoms: After blooming, the plant doesn't produce offsets.
conditions.remedy: Pups can take months; keep up normal care and wait.
conditions.prevention: Feed with bromeliad fertilizer monthly in growing season.

common_conditions: brown center from water sitting in base
sunburn from direct midday sun through glass
lore: Hailed as the 'queen of air plants' for its size and sculpted silver rosette. Listed under CITES Appendix II due to overcollection in the wild — only nursery-grown specimens may be traded internationally.
etymology: From Greek 'xeros' (dry) and 'graphis' (writing, drawing) — the dry, curling silvery leaves look like calligraphic strokes.

common_conditions: rot from staying wet against a surface
lore: Neither Spanish nor moss — it is a bromeliad, and French colonists nicknamed it 'Spanish beard' to mock the Spanish; the Spanish replied with 'French hair'. Used as mattress stuffing across the American South for centuries.
etymology: 'Usneoides' means 'resembling Usnea', the unrelated lichen it mimics in form.

light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Bleached or brown strands in harsh sun

placement_tips: Hang with excellent airflow — never against a wall
Mist daily in dry rooms or dunk 5 min every 3 days
Bright spot away from cold drafts
Humidity ≥50% preferred

conditions.name: Browning from stagnant air
conditions.symptoms: Strands turn brown and brittle where airflow is poor.
conditions.remedy: Relocate to an airier spot; trim dead sections.
conditions.prevention: Hang with good airflow; avoid closed corners.

conditions.name: Thinning from too little water
conditions.symptoms: The clump thins out and loses its lush grey-green.
conditions.remedy: Mist daily or dunk for 5 min every 3 days.
conditions.prevention: Mist daily in dry rooms; more frequent care than ionantha.

conditions.name: Rot against a wet surface
conditions.symptoms: Sections touching a wet wall or soil turn brown.
conditions.remedy: Move off damp surfaces.
conditions.prevention: Hang free; never against anything that holds moisture.

conditions.name: Fading in low light
conditions.symptoms: Loses the silvery-grey colour, turns dull.
conditions.remedy: Move to brighter indirect light.
conditions.prevention: Bright indirect; a little filtered direct sun is fine.

conditions.name: Chunks falling off
conditions.symptoms: Sections of the clump drop to the floor.
conditions.remedy: Re-hang the fallen pieces; they continue growing separately.
conditions.prevention: Natural propagation; every dropped chunk is a new plant.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

conditions.name: Fungus gnats
conditions.symptoms: Small black flies rising from the soil when disturbed; larvae in the top layer.
conditions.remedy: Let the topsoil dry out fully; apply yellow sticky traps; treat with BTI (mosquito bits) if persistent.
conditions.prevention: Avoid constantly wet topsoil; bottom-water when possible.

conditions.name: Spider mites
conditions.symptoms: Fine webbing under leaves, stippled yellow speckling, leaves turn dull.
conditions.remedy: Rinse under the shower, wipe leaves with neem oil or insecticidal soap weekly for 3 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Keep humidity above 50%; inspect new plants before bringing them home.

conditions.name: Mealybugs
conditions.symptoms: White cottony clumps in leaf joints and along stems; sticky residue on leaves.
conditions.remedy: Dab each bug with a cotton swab dipped in 70% isopropyl alcohol; follow up weekly until clear.
conditions.prevention: Quarantine new plants for 2 weeks; wipe leaves monthly.

conditions.name: Scale insects
conditions.symptoms: Small brown bumps stuck to stems or leaf undersides; sticky honeydew underneath.
conditions.remedy: Scrape off by hand, then treat with neem oil every 7 days for a month.
conditions.prevention: Inspect leaf undersides monthly, especially after bringing new plants home.

conditions.name: Fungal leaf spot
conditions.symptoms: Dark or yellow-haloed spots on leaves, sometimes with a concentric pattern.
conditions.remedy: Remove affected leaves, improve airflow, avoid wetting foliage, apply a copper-based fungicide if widespread.
conditions.prevention: Water at the soil, not on leaves; keep plants from crowding each other.

conditions.name: Overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Yellowing leaves that feel soft, consistently damp soil, base of stem blackening.
conditions.remedy: Stop watering, move to brighter spot, check drainage holes, repot if the soil stays wet >10 days.
conditions.prevention: Check soil moisture with your finger before watering; use a pot with drainage.

conditions.name: Underwatering
conditions.symptoms: Crispy leaf edges, drooping, soil pulling away from the pot sides.
conditions.remedy: Soak the pot in a basin for 15 minutes to rehydrate the rootball, then resume a regular schedule.
conditions.prevention: Set a watering reminder; don't skip more than one scheduled watering in a row.

conditions.name: Cold draft damage
conditions.symptoms: Sudden leaf drop, black patches on foliage near a window or door.
conditions.remedy: Move the plant away from drafts and heating vents; trim damaged leaves once growth resumes.
conditions.prevention: Keep plants at least 30 cm from single-glazed windows in winter and away from exterior doors.

conditions.name: Nutrient deficiency
conditions.symptoms: Uniformly pale leaves, weak new growth, older leaves yellowing first.
conditions.remedy: Flush the pot with plain water, then resume feeding with a balanced liquid fertilizer at half strength.
conditions.prevention: Feed every 2–4 weeks during the growing season; refresh the top 2 cm of soil annually.

care.placement_hints: Mount with base slightly elevated so water runs off; bulb-base tolerates dry.

common_conditions: rot in the hollow bulb base from trapped water
loss of leaves from extreme dryness
lore: Named for the Greek Gorgon: writhing silver-green tendrils emerge from a bulbous base like snakes from a severed head. The hollow base is often colonised by ants in the wild in a mutually beneficial arrangement.
etymology: 'Caput-medusae' is Latin for 'head of Medusa'.

light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Bleached leaf tips in intense afternoon sun

placement_tips: Mount with the base slightly tilted down so water runs off
Bright spot with airflow
Soak 15–20 min weekly; shake out thoroughly
Never mount in moss that holds moisture against the bulb

conditions.name: Rot in the hollow bulb
conditions.symptoms: Bulb base turns soft and discoloured.
conditions.remedy: Too late to save if deep; propagate any pups.
conditions.prevention: Mount with the base tilted down; never soak upright.

conditions.name: Curling leaves from underwatering
conditions.symptoms: Tentacle leaves curl tightly and look silvery.
conditions.remedy: Soak for 20 minutes; uncurls within hours.
conditions.prevention: Weekly 15–20 min soaks; shake out thoroughly.

conditions.name: Pale colour in low light
conditions.symptoms: Loses the rich green-grey tone.
conditions.remedy: Move to bright indirect.
conditions.prevention: Bright indirect light; medusae tolerate some direct morning sun.

conditions.name: Leaf loss from extreme dryness
conditions.symptoms: Tentacle leaves break off when very dry.
conditions.remedy: Soak thoroughly; new leaves replace them.
conditions.prevention: Never stretch past 2 weeks without water.

conditions.name: No pups
conditions.symptoms: After blooming, no new pups form.
conditions.remedy: Wait — can take 6 months; keep up normal care.
conditions.prevention: Feed with bromeliad fertilizer monthly during growing season.
